coverage_notes: Strong coverage across early specialization risks, youth injury epidemiology (including running-specific), LTAD physiological evidence, youth resistance training consensus, and adolescent distance runner training adaptations. Minor gap in longitudinal studies tracking youth runners into adult performance, and limited RCTs specific to youth distance running interventions.

authors: Council on Sports Medicine and Fitness
papers.title: Sports Specialization and Intensive Training in Young Athletes
papers.notes: AAP clinical report providing evidence-based guidance on sport specialization and intensive training in young athletes. Concludes that delaying sport specialization until after puberty (~15-16 years) minimizes injury risk and psychological burnout while maximizing likelihood of athletic success. Recommends limiting single-sport participation to 5 days/week with 2-3 months off per year.

papers.title: Sports specialization in young athletes: evidence-based recommendations
papers.notes: Comprehensive evidence-based review of risks and benefits of early sport specialization. Identifies higher rates of overuse injury, increased psychological stress, and earlier sport dropout as key risks. Recommends sport diversification and limiting organized sport participation hours to less than the child's age per week. Foundational paper for youth sport specialization guidelines.

papers.title: Impact of youth sports specialisation on career and task-specific athletic performance: a systematic review following the American Medical Society for Sports Medicine (AMSSM) Collaborative Research Network's 2019 Youth Early Sport Specialisation Summit
papers.notes: Systematic review from the AMSSM summit finding that early sport specialization is not required for elite-level success and does not improve task-related performance compared to diversified athletes during childhood and adolescence. Sport diversification appears to be a better path for both athletic success and minimized injury risk.

papers.title: The association between early specialization and performance level with injury and illness risk in youth elite athletes
papers.journal: Scandinavian Journal of Medicine & Science in Sports
papers.notes: Prospective cohort study of 259 Norwegian youth elite athletes examining the association between early specialization, performance level, and injury/illness risk. Found that early specialization was significantly associated with higher total injury count (most commonly overuse injuries) and increased time missed from sport, supporting recommendations for sport diversification in youth.

papers.title: The long-term athlete development model: physiological evidence and application
papers.notes: Comprehensive review of the physiological evidence underpinning the LTAD model. Examines windows of trainability for aerobic fitness, strength, speed, and flexibility across developmental stages. Provides evidence-based framework for age-appropriate training prescription, identifying sensitive periods for different physical capacities during growth and maturation. Essential reference for structuring youth running programs.

papers.title: Position statement on youth resistance training: the 2014 International Consensus
papers.notes: International consensus statement from 20 experts across sports medicine and exercise science. Concludes that appropriately designed and supervised youth resistance training is safe and effective for enhancing muscular strength, motor skills, and injury resilience. Directly relevant to youth runners as it supports inclusion of strength work at all developmental stages when properly programmed.

papers.title: Physeal injuries in children's and youth sports: reasons for concern?
papers.notes: Comprehensive review of growth plate (physeal) injury risks in youth sports. The young athlete with open growth plates is vulnerable to unique overuse injuries involving apophyses, articular cartilage, and physis. Discusses mechanisms of physeal injury from repetitive loading in running, risk factors during growth spurts, and implications for training load management in young distance runners.

papers.title: Pediatric running injuries
papers.notes: Running-specific review of pediatric injury patterns. Covers apophyseal injuries (Sever's disease, Osgood-Schlatter), stress fractures, and overuse conditions unique to growing runners. Addresses the vulnerability of open growth plates to repetitive loading and provides age-appropriate training guidelines for youth distance running, including mileage recommendations.

papers.title: Youth Distance Running and Lower Extremity Injury: A Systematic Review
papers.journal: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
papers.notes: First systematic review specifically investigating youth distance running (<15 years) and lower extremity injury. Included 9 studies (2 cohort, 7 case reports/series). Found youth can participate in distance running with minimal adverse effects, though growth plate vulnerability is a concern. Middle school runners had fewer injuries (14.2%) than high school runners (20.8%). Identified a significant paucity of research, especially for children under 10.

papers.title: Injuries and Training Practices in Competitive Adolescent Distance Runners: A Retrospective Cross-Sectional Study
papers.journal: Frontiers in Sports and Active Living
papers.notes: Cross-sectional study of 113 competitive adolescent distance runners (ages 13-18) from England Athletics clubs. Found 68% injury incidence proportion over 12 months with an injury rate of 6.3/1000 participation hours. Most common injuries were knee, foot/toes, and lower leg from overuse. Training frequency increased significantly with age, and 58% demonstrated high specialization levels. Directly relevant data for coaching adolescent runners.
